title: The Macro Minute: Is the US consumer helping or hurting our Paradigm C bull market?

Happy Friday out there team42 at your
skipper here Darius Delta to present our
macro minute for Friday September 26
2025. As always we'll start with the
executive summary from today's lid off
morning note. If you would like to
review the analysis supporting these
conclusions and to see if you need to
make any changes in your portfolio to
remain on the right side of market risk
or to get on the right side of market
risk to the extent you haven't been
following KISS or Dr. Mo need to be a
client of 42 macro. So today's macro
question is is the US consumer helping
or hurting our paradigm C bull market.
Our answer, this is fast developing into
the $64 trillion question given the
recent backup in Treasury yields and
decline in near-term Fed policy
normalization expectations.
Indeed, the strong positive impulses and
headline goods PC growth, stabilization
and services PC growth, and declining
personal savings rate in the August PC
report supported our resilient US
economy theme. The strong negative
impulse in nominal disposable personal
uh income growth in the August BC report
supported our U-shaped economy thesis.
The strong negative impulse in nominal
wage and salary compensation growth in
the August BC report also supported our
U-shaped economy thesis. Recall that
nominal wage and salary compensation is
the broadest measure of income we
receive from the labor market on a
monthly frequency. As such, three
consecutive months of below trend growth
for the first time since January of 2023
is bad and supportive of our U-shaped
economy thesis. The weak positive
impulses in headline core and super core
inflation in the August PC report
supported our sticky inflation theme.
Reminder, inflation is by far the most
lagging indicator of the business cycle
and is unlikely to return durably to
trend without an actual recession. This
dynamic is somewhat derisked because the
Fed is already expecting inflation to
accelerate over the next one to two
quarters. Moreover, Wall Street
consensus is expecting inflation to
accelerate over the next one to two
quarters. Wall Street consensus is also
expecting inflation to remain sticky
over the next 1 to two years.
Further good news is for investors is
that our sticky inflation theme is fully
priced on a 1 to two year basis and
fairly priced on a 5 to 10 year basis.
Additionally, what if AI is structurally
disinflationary as many expect it to be?
Rates are headed much lower if the next
Fed chair credibly weaves this into the
debate surrounding the neutral rate. And
commentary from key thought leaders like
Rick Reer, Kevin Walsh, David Malpass,
Mark Summerland, and others supports our
call for regime change at the Fed. Note,
each of these men are on pus' short list
of potential power replacements.
Deepening deflation in nationwide home
prices, the largest weight in CPI and
tied for the largest weight in core PCE
is an argument we anticipate will gain
more traction in the coming months as
well. Transitioning to our 42 micro
dashboard as always wrap up the question
from our community. This one's titled

for anyone who takes the time to answer

equities, fixed income sectors, and
macro exposures. Uh, you can just click
on any one of the charts. The uh, dark
green bar, the dark green shaded area
represents a long max position as
signaled by our discretionary manage aka
Dr. Mo. The light green signals a uh
long half position as indicated by Dr.
Mo. The gray represents a neutral uh
sorry a no position. That means don't be
in the asset at the current juncture. No
position equals gray. Red equals short
max position. Uh and uh orange uh
there's no um short half positions for
uh for the um for the fix for the uh
equity sectors. Let me just find
something that might have an orange
here.
Most things don't have short half
positions because uh markets for our
institutional understanding of how
markets function is that they V-bottom
and so a lot of times we see when we
programmed the back test we recognized
that a lot of the uh positive
performance came in short half positions
when markets reverse and debottom. So we
just programmed that logic out of uh the
Dr. Mo signals for equities um cry
cryptocurrencies commodities uh and
credit all the all the sort of risk
asset exposures within Dr. mode don't
have short half exposures uh for that

only capturing the downside uh risk
associated with going into the crash as
opposed to being late in the crash, you
know, kind of um and getting uh uh you
know, short squeezed and once the Fed
starts to ease and we get those V bottom
explosive moves. So, I just want
everybody to be aware of that. So,
again, as I mentioned, just you know,

what the cut volatility just a momentum

that is powering our global macro matrix

portfolio here in the macro minute, but

since we're going through uh the signal
section of the dashboard. So uh when you

signal section you can uh see the gross
exposure how we're currently allocated
and this is a function of the top down
and bottom up percentage overlays that
we feature in KISS to formulate this
gross exposure. Here you can see the
percentage of maximum exposure uh right
here. So we're at 100% of our maximum
exposure 60% in stocks. We're 100% of
our maximum exposure 30% in gold. We're
100% of our maximum exposure of 10% in
Bitcoin and have been uh for a couple
quarters now. Uh not sorry not a couple
quarters several months now. uh if you
click on the percent of maximum exposure
tab, it'll turn into the beta adjusted
S&P delta. So you can kind of toggle
that back and forth to see. And so it
gives you a sense of what this current
portfolio configuration is in units of
S&P based on the beta of these
particular asset class, the beta and the
correlation of these asset classes
relative to the S&P. So a 30% position
in gold is essentially a minus 0.3%
position in in the S&P 500. this 10%
position in Bitcoin is is currently
functioning as an 8% position in S&P
500. Again, these change over time based
on the trailing betas and correlations
to the S&P 500. And then obviously a 60%
position in SPLG is is essentially a 60%
position uh in uh the S&P 500. Uh and
then if you click on these um these tabs
as well, you'll bring up the pop-up so
you can see it a little bit more
clearly. As someone who's getting uh
close to 40, I'm starting to uh I'm
noticing myself leaning a little bit
closer into my computer screen uh every
morning. So, I'm guessing my eyesight is
starting to wne. So, you can definitely

here. Again, percent max exposure beta
adjusted S&P 500 delta and then the
overall um allocations uh current
allocations for the KISS u portfolio.
